proposed_description: Adjectives ending in -cio drop the unstressed -i when forming the masculine plural: vecchio → vecchi (not vecchii), liscio → lisci. Reliable when the -i of -cio is unstressed.
rationale: Needed for items targeting common A1/A2 adjectives vecchio and liscio. The current tree has no place for this; the closest existing leaf is co_chi, which is wrong because there is no h insertion here.

proposed_description: Adjectives ending in -gio drop the unstressed -i in the masculine plural: grigio → grigi, saggio → saggi. Mirrors -cio → -ci.
rationale: Same reasoning as cio_ci. Grigio is a high-frequency colour adjective, students hit this early.

proposed_description: Feminine plural of -cia adjectives drops the unstressed -i: liscia → lisce. The rule is sometimes stated as 'the -i is preserved only if stressed' (farmacia → farmacie keeps it because of stress, but farmacia is a noun). For adjectives the unstressed case dominates.
rationale: Required for any item testing feminine plural of vecchio (vecchie), liscio (lisce). Common at A2.

proposed_description: Feminine plural of -gia adjectives drops the unstressed -i: grigia → grige (note: most native speakers also accept grigie; this is a known wobble). Saggia → sagge.
rationale: Companion to cia_ce. Worth flagging that this one has dual-form acceptance, so authoring needs care on must_not_include.

proposed_description: Despite penultimate stress (which would predict -chi), amico forms its masculine plural as amici with soft c. Word-specific irregular. Feminine plural is regular: amiche.
rationale: Amico is one of the most common adjectives/nouns; it is also the canonical exception to the co_chi rule. Without this leaf the marker cannot distinguish 'forgot to apply -chi' from 'applied -chi where amico needs -ci'.

proposed_description: Greco takes -ci in the masculine plural (greci) despite stress that would predict -chi. Feminine plural is greche (regular).
rationale: Greco appears frequently in cultural/historical content from A2 onward.

proposed_label: antico → antichi (regular -chi where -ci might be expected)
proposed_description: Antico takes -chi in the masculine plural (antichi) even though the stress is on the antepenult, which by the predictable rule (see stem_changes.harder.co_ci_vs_chi_stress) would suggest -ci. Counter-irregular: it follows the default rule despite stress. Useful for distinguishing rule knowledge from rote knowledge.
rationale: The classic counter-example to simpatico → simpatici. Worth a leaf because it inverts the stress rule and is high-frequency in cultural/historical contexts.

proposed_description: When an adjective qualifies a mixed-gender plural noun phrase (e.g. 'ragazzi e ragazze'), the adjective takes the masculine plural form: 'i ragazzi e le ragazze italiani'. Cross-class rule; applies to o_class and e_class alike.

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: i ragazzi e le ragazze sono italiani

rationale: A real-world common miss that the current tree has no leaf for. Cross-cuts both classes, so it sits at the top of adjective_agreement rather than under one class.

proposed_description: Adjectives that do not change form for gender or number. Includes some colour terms borrowed from nouns (blu, rosa, viola, marrone, beige), compound colour expressions (verde scuro, blu marino), and a small set of fixed forms (pari, dispari, impari).
rationale: Currently completely absent from the tree. Invariable colour adjectives are an early miss point (learners over-agree). Needs to be a sibling of o_class and e_class.

proposed_label: Invariable colour adjectives derived from nouns
proposed_description: Colours that derive from nouns and do not inflect: blu, rosa, viola, marrone, beige, fucsia, lilla, indaco. Arancione is variable in modern usage but historically invariable; treat as edge case. 'Le case rosa' not 'le case rose'.
Two-word colour expressions are invariable as a unit: verde scuro, blu marino, giallo limone, rosso fuoco, azzurro cielo. 'Le pareti verde scuro' not 'verdi scure'.

proposed_attributes.common_miss: verdi scure (full agreement) or verde scure (half agreement)

rationale: Often missed at B1+. The invariability of compounds is the rule, not the exception.

proposed_label: pari, dispari, impari and similar invariables
proposed_description: A small group of mathematically/structurally defined adjectives that do not inflect: pari (even, equal), dispari (odd, unequal), impari (uneven). 'I numeri pari', 'le squadre pari'.
rationale: Small but real bucket. Comes up in maths and scheduling contexts.

proposed_description: un vecchio amico = a long-standing friend; un amico vecchio = a friend who is elderly. The pre-noun reading is figurative/relational, the post-noun reading is literal/physical.

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: un vecchio amico vs un amico vecchio

rationale: Re-proposed 2026-05-14 with item set attached. Grammar items adj_pos_shift_vecchio_01, _02, _03 forward-reference this leaf and demonstrate diagnostic coverage of both readings.

proposed_description: un grande uomo = a great man (figurative); un uomo grande = a tall/big man (literal).

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: un grande uomo vs un uomo grande

rationale: Re-proposed 2026-05-14 with item set attached. Grammar items adj_pos_shift_grande_01, _02, _03 forward-reference this leaf. Note: the existing adj_pos_shift_02 and _03 (against the aggregate) cover similar ground and the architect may want to migrate those to point at this leaf when promoted.

proposed_description: il povero ragazzo = the poor (unfortunate) boy; un ragazzo povero = a boy who has little money.

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: il povero ragazzo vs un ragazzo povero

rationale: Re-proposed 2026-05-14 with item set attached. Grammar items adj_pos_shift_povero_01, _02, _03 forward-reference this leaf. The existing adj_pos_shift_05 (against the aggregate) covers similar ground and could migrate.

_meta_note_for_architect: The architecture chat should also fix a typo in adjective_agreement.e_class.feminine_plural.attributes.common_miss. The current string reads 'case grande (no number) or case grande (Class I confusion)'. The second 'case grande' should read 'case grandi' (the Class I confusion: a learner over-applying -i because they expect Class I rather than Class II).
